query: blaCTX-M-15 ESBL cephalosporin resistance Klebsiella pneumoniae
question: How does blaCTX-M-15 confer resistance in Klebsiella pneumoniae, which antibiotics are affected, and what are the recommended treatment implications?
answer:
The blaCTX-M-15 gene encodes an extended-spectrum β-lactamase (ESBL) that has become the globally dominant ESBL variant in Enterobacterales, including Klebsiella pneumoniae (cantón2012ctxmenzymesorigin pages 3-5). CTX-M-15 efficiently hydrolyzes third-generation cephalosporins such as ceftriaxone, cefotaxime, and ceftazidime, and confers clinically significant resistance to cefepime (peirano2019extendedspectrumbetalactamaseproducing pages 12-14). Aminopenicillins and their β-lactamase-inhibitor combinations (amoxicillin-clavulanate, ampicillin-sulbactam) frequently fail in CTX-M-15-producing K. pneumoniae because clavulanate inhibition is often insufficient in Klebsiella species (livermore2008ctxmchangingthe pages 4-6).

Currently accepted clinical practice for serious infections caused by ESBL-producing Enterobacterales prioritizes carbapenems (meropenem or ertapenem) as first-line therapy, based on the MERINO trial and subsequent guidance from the IDSA (harris2018meropenemversuspiperacillin pages 1-4, tamma2023infectiousdiseasessociety pages 8-12). Piperacillin-tazobactam is now discouraged for CTX-M-producing bloodstream infections due to inferior mortality outcomes. When carbapenem-sparing therapy is desired, aminoglycosides retaining susceptibility (typically amikacin) may play a role in urinary sources.

citations.title: CTX-M Enzymes: Origin and Diffusion
citations.citation: Rafael Cantón, José María González-Alba, and Juan Carlos Galán. CTX-M enzymes: origin and diffusion. Frontiers in Microbiology, 3:110, 2012.
citations.excerpt: CTX-M-15 has become the most widely distributed ESBL globally, disseminating through both hospital and community reservoirs, and now dominates the ESBL landscape in Enterobacterales including Klebsiella pneumoniae and Escherichia coli.

citations.title: Effect of Piperacillin-Tazobactam vs Meropenem on 30-Day Mortality for Patients With E coli or Klebsiella pneumoniae Bloodstream Infection and Ceftriaxone Resistance
citations.citation: Patrick N. A. Harris et al. Effect of piperacillin-tazobactam vs meropenem on 30-day mortality for patients with E coli or Klebsiella pneumoniae bloodstream infection and ceftriaxone resistance: the MERINO randomized clinical trial. JAMA, 320(10):984-994, 2018.
citations.excerpt: In patients with ceftriaxone-resistant E. coli or K. pneumoniae bloodstream infection, piperacillin-tazobactam did not meet criteria for noninferiority compared with meropenem; carbapenems remain the preferred definitive therapy.

citations.title: Infectious Diseases Society of America 2023 Guidance on the Treatment of Antimicrobial Resistant Gram-Negative Infections
citations.citation: Pranita D. Tamma et al. Infectious Diseases Society of America 2023 guidance on the treatment of antimicrobial resistant gram-negative infections. Clinical Infectious Diseases, ciad428, 2023.
citations.excerpt: For ESBL-producing Enterobacterales causing bloodstream or serious infections, a carbapenem (meropenem or ertapenem) is the preferred treatment; piperacillin-tazobactam is no longer recommended as first-line therapy.
